coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of novel organic-inorganic hybrid fluorescent microspheres and their applications as Fe(III), Hg(II) and biothiols probes
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hao
Nie, Chen
Wang, Jiahui
Guan, Ruifang
Cao, Duxia
coredata.dc:description: Organic-inorganic hybrid fluorescent microspheres (OI-FMs) without typical fluorophores were prepared. Fluorescence quantum yield of OI-FMs is up to 70.0% after being heated in an oven at 120 °C (named as OI-FMs-120). Interestingly, OI-FMs-120 shows different detection performances in different solvents. It can be used for qualitative and quantitative detection of Fe3 + with a detection limit of 37.8 nM in absolute ethanol. While in aqueous solution, it can be applied as an ON-OFF-ON fluorescent probe. Fluorescence of the probe is quenched by Hg2 + and later recovered by biothiols. The detection limit is as low as 34.9 nM for Hg2 +, 91.0 nM for Cys, 0.12 μM for Hcy and 0.13 μM for GSH in aqueous medium, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Atypical fluorophores
Fluorescence microspheres
Heavy metal ions
Biothiols

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescent small molecule CA-AEAPMDS
Preparation of fluorescent organic-inorganic hybrid microspheres OI- FMs
Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ in aqueous medium
Optimizing the experimental conditions for Hg2+ detection
Selectivity of OI-FMs-120 for Hg2+ detection
Sensitivity of OI-FMs-120 for Hg2+ detection
Mechanism of the detection of biothiols
Specificity of OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ system for biothiols
Sensitivity of OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ system for biothiols
Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ in anhydrous ethanol
Selectivity of OI-FMs-120 for Fe3+ detection
Sensitivity of OI-FMs-120 for Fe3+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) IR spectrum and (b) SEM pattern of OI-FMs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, optimum excitation spectrum and maximum emission spectrum of OI-FMs (b) Emission spectra of OI-FMs at different excitation wavelengths.
OI-FMs samples were heated in ovens of different temperatures for 2 h. λex = 360 nm.

Selectivity of OI-FMs-120 solution towards 50 μM Hg2+ (blue bars), and interference of 50 μM of other metal ions with 50 μM Hg2+ (green bars) in aqueous solution at pH 7.0. 1, blank; 2, Fe3+ (50 μM); 3, Hg2+ (50 μM); 4, Fe2+ (50 μM); 5, Cu2+ (50 μM); 6, Ca2+ (50 μM); 7, Ag+ (50 μM); 8, Ni+ (50 μM); 9, Al3+ (50 μM); 10, Zn2+ (50 μM); 11, Na+ (50 μM); 12, K+ (50 μM); 13, Sn2+ (50 μM); 14, Cd2+ (50 μM); 15, Co2+ (50 μM); 16, Mg2+ (50 μM); 17, Cr2+ (50 μM). λex = 360 nm.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of OI-FMs-120 in the presence of Hg2+ with different concentrations; (b) linear relationship between I0/I within the Hg2+ concentrations in the range from 0 to 50 μM. I0 and I are the emission fluorescence intensities of OI-FMs-120 at 450 nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+.

(a) Selectivity of OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ system toward 50 μM biothiols (green bars), and interference of 50 μM of other amino acids with 50 μM Cys (purple bars) in aqueous solution at pH 7.0; (b) PL spectra of OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ system in presence of GSH, Hcy or Cys with a concentration of 50 μM.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ in the presence of Cys with different concentrations; (b) linear relationship between I0/I within the Cys concentrations in the range from 0–50 μM. (I0 and I are the emission fluorescence intensities of OI-FMs-120 at 450 nm in the presence and absence of Cys, respectively).

Selectivity of OI-FMs-120 solution towards 50 μM Fe3+(black bars), and interference of 50 μM of other metal ions with 50 μM Fe3+ (bad bars) in anhydrous ethanol. 1, blank; 2, Hg2+ (50 μM); 3, Ag+ (50 μM); 4, Fe2+ (50 μM); 5, Cu2+ (50 μM); 6, Ca2+ (50 μM); 7,Fe3+ (50 μM); 8, Ni+ (50 μM); 9, Al3+ (50 μM); 10, Zn2+ (50 μM); 11, Na+ (50 μM); 12, K+ (50 μM); 13, Sn2+ (50 μM); 14, Cd2+ (50 μM); 15, Co2+ (50 μM); 16, Mg2+ (50 μM); 17, Cr2+ (50 μM). λex = 360 nm.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of OI-FMs-120 in the presence of Fe3+ with different concentrations; (b) linear relationship between I0/I and the Fe3+ concentrations in the range from 0 to 75 μM.
Illustration of the steps involved in the synthesis of organic-inorganic hybrid fluorescent microspheres and the detection and sensing of heavy metal ions and biothiols.
Performance comparison of different fluorescent sensors for the detection of Hg2+.
Determination of Hg2+ in tap water and river water (n = 3).
Performance comparison of different fluorescent sensors for the detection of Fe3+.
Determination of Fe3+ in tap water and river water (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of novel organic-inorganic hybrid fluorescent microspheres and their applications as Fe(III), Hg(II) and biothiols probes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organic-inorganic hybrid fluorescent microspheres (OI-FMs) without typical fluorophores were prepared. Fluorescence quantum yield of OI-FMs is up to 70.0% after being heated in an oven at 120 °C (named as OI-FMs-120). Interestingly, OI-FMs-120 shows different detection performances in different solvents. It can be used for qualitative and quantitative detection of Fe3 + with a detection limit of 37.8 nM in absolute ethanol. While in aqueous solution, it can be applied as an ON-OFF-ON fluorescent probe. Fluorescence of the probe is quenched by Hg2 + and later recovered by biothiols. The detection limit is as low as 34.9 nM for Hg2 +, 91.0 nM for Cys, 0.12 μM for Hcy and 0.13 μM for GSH in aqueous medium, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Immobilizing organic fluorescent molecules on a solid substrate is a normal way to prepare organic-inorganic hybrid solid-state fluorescence sensor. These solid substrates generally include SiO2 [1–3], TiO2 [4], Fe3O4 [2,5] and polymer films [6–8], which have improved photo-stability, photo-bleaching resistance than traditional organic fluorescent molecular sensors [1,9,10]. Silica has high porosity, tunable pore size, large specific surface area and good thermal stability, which make it a good solid carrier for organic fluorescent probe molecules [11,12]. Organic-inorganic hybrid silica fluorescence sensors have drawn much attention for their wide applications in detection, bioanalysis, multi-targeting detection systems, in vitro and in vivo imaging, and drug delivery [13–16].

In most literatures, traditional organic fluorescent compounds were used as fluorescent sources to prepare organic-inorganic hybrid silica materials. Electronic conjugate and molecular rigid structures are essential in traditional organic fluorescent compounds [16–18], while they generally have poor water solubility, low biocompatibility and high toxicity, which limit their application in biological field. In recent years, some nitrogen-containing organic compounds without typical fluorophores were found to be fluorescent, good water-soluble, biocompatible and low toxic, which arouse researchers’ interest [19,20]. In our previous work, we synthesized fluorescent amide compound CA-AEAPMDS [21] by citric acid (CA) and N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMDS), CA-AEAPMDS has good biocompatibility and water solubility and its fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is as high as 40.6%. In this paper, two reactive silicon hydroxyl groups being involved at the end of CA-AEAPMDS reacted with the silicon ethoxy groups of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), then further fluorescent organic-inorganic hybrid silica sensors were constructed by the Stöber method [22]. Compared with frequently-used post-grafting methods [23–27], organic groups introduced by this way are uniformly dispersed within the whole skeleton rather than existing over the outer surface, orifice of the material and then blocking pores. To the best of our knowledge, it was the first time that fluorescent compounds without typical fluorophores were applied to prepare fluorescent organic-inorganic hybrid microspheres. Particularly, it is multifunctional probes in various solvent. It can be used for qualitative and quantitative detection of Fe3+ in absolute ethanol, while in aqueous solution, it was applied as an ON-OFF-ON probe: quenched by Hg2+ and later recovered by biothiols.

Hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, anhydrous ethanol, tetraethoxysilane, petroleum ether, aqueous ammonia, n-hexane, citric acid, thionyl chloride (SOCl2), tetrahydrofuran and AEAPMDS were purchased from Aladdin company (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent small molecule CA-AEAPMDS
ce:section.ce:para: According to references [21], fluorescent small molecule CA-AEAPMDS was prepared as detailed steps. A certain amount of anhydrous citric acid was dissolved in tetrahydrofuran, and an appropriate amount of thionyl chloride was added into the solution (nCA: nSOCl2 = 1: 3.2). Then the mixed solution was stirred at 0 °C for 12 h. Next, the product of the first step was purified by rotary evaporation three times, and then dissolved in proper amount of tetrahydrofuran. AEAPMDS (n CA: n AEAPMDS = 1:3.2) was added into mixture with stirring. After the reaction was carried out for 3 h, the product was washed three times with petroleum ether. After vacuum filtration, the desired final product was obtained. The structure of CA-AEAPMDS was confirmed by 1H NMR (Fig. S1) and HRMS (Fig. S2) spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent organic-inorganic hybrid microspheres OI- FMs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent silica microspheres were synthesized by the Stöber method [22] ( Scheme 1), 100 mL of absolute ethanol, 10 mL of water and 3 mL of aqueous ammonia (28%) were added to the round bottom flask successively and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. Then 0.05 g of CA-AEAPMDS and 1 mL of TEOS were slowly added dropwise to the mixed solution under stirring, and then the reaction was sealed for 6 h. After the reaction was finished, the mixture was washed three times with deionized water and absolute ethanol in the end, the final product was heat-treated in an oven at 60 °C, 80 °C, 100 °C, 120 °C, 140 °C, 160 °C, 180 °C, 200 °C and 220 °C for two hours, respectively. Example at 120 °C is named OI- FMs-120.

All fluorescence tests were performed at room temperature and the excitation wavelength was set to 360 nm. First, 2 mL of OI-FMs-120 buffer solution (2 μg mL−1) was added to a quartz cuvette, and then a certain amount of the solution to be tested was added. The mixed solution was shaken for a while, and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded. The concentrations of Fe3+ and Hg2+ used in the sensitivity experiments were 0–75.0 μM and 0–50.0 μM, respectively. A similar procedure was followed for detection of other metal ions. To assess the Cys sensitivity, a 40 μM mixed solution of Hg2+ and OI-FMs-120 (2 μg mL−1) was prepared at pH 7.0 and the solution was sonicated for 2 min before spectroscopic measurement. The detection range is from 0 to 50 μM and other amino acids experiments follow similar procedures.

Tap water samples were collected from our laboratory and river water samples from Jinan. Then, the collected water samples were reprocessed to avoid interference with the testing process. Firstly, the collected water and tap water samples were left at room temperature for a day to remove the sediment. Then a 0.45 μm filter was used to remove small particles from the upper water sample. Finally, water samples are collected and stored in glass bottles at 4 ℃ for use.

UV–vis spectra were determined on a Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were captured using a Horiba Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrometer. Nuclear magnetic resonance spectra were measured on a MercuryPlus-400 spectrometer. Mass spectra (ESI) were gathered on a LQC system (Finngan MAT, USA). Specific surface area and pore distribution were obtained by nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms using a specific surface area analyzer (QuadraSorb Station 2). Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was perform on a analyzer (TA, SDTQ600) from 20 to 790 ℃ at a rate of 20 ℃ min−1 under nitrogen atmosphere. Scanning electron microscope (SEM, QUANTA FEG 250, USA) was employed to characterize microstructure and morphology. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded on NICOLET-iS50 spectrometer to characterize chemical groups. The XPS spectra measurements were performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (ESCALAB 250XI).

In order to demonstrate the successful synthesis of organic-inorganic hybrid fluorescent microspheres, FT-IR spectrum of the samples were measured, as shown in Fig. 1a. The typical strong bands at 1100 cm−1 and 460 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching and bending vibrations of Si-O-Si indicate the formation of silica network structure. The peaks at 2850 cm−1 and 2940 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-H bonds. The peak at 1550 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of C-H and N-H, meanwhile and the peak at 1700 cm−1 corresponds to C=O stretching vibration. These results confirm the binding of CA-AEAPMDS in the backbone of fluorescent silica microspheres. In addition, the peak at 3440 cm−1 was caused by the deformation and stretching vibration of Si-OH and N-H on the surface of the fluorescent silica microspheres, which indicates that the fluorescent microspheres have good water solubility.

SEM image is shown in Fig. 1b. The particle size of OI-FMs is about 200 nm with uniform size. The surface area and pore structures of OI-FMs were measured by N2 adsorption. Fig. S3 shows the adsorption-desorption curve of OI-FMs. The hysteresis loop is nearly vertical and very high, while the overlap also relatively flat, indicating that the hole distribution OI-FMs is more even. The surface area is 95.02 m2 / g, and the average pore size is about 1.006 nm (Fig. S3). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement of OI-FMs reveals peaks from the C1s, N1s, O1s, O2s, Si2s, and Si2p core levels (Fig. S4a). The C1s spectrum (Fig. S4c) shows three peaks at 284.6, 286.15 and 288.25 eV which are attributed to C-C, C-N/C-O and C=O respectively. The presence of C=O signal, along with FTIR data, further reveals the existence of CA-AEAPMDS in the surfaces of OI-FMs.

The TGA curve with three major weight-loss processes is shown in (Fig. S5). The first loss-in-weight process is at 100 °C with a weight loss of approximately 5.5% which is classified as the residual water and anhydrous ethanol in preparation of OI-FMs and desorption of physically adsorbed water in the pore. The second weight loss process is from 100 °C to 250 °C, with a loss of approximately 6.3%, due to the loss of crystalline water and the further condensation of unhydrolyzed Si-OCH3 and Si-OH. The third weight loss process is from 250 °C to 800 °C, the corresponding weight loss is 6.2%, due to the decomposition of organic groups of CA-AEAPMDS at high temperature. The above thermogravimetric results show that the OI-FMs material has good thermal stability.

The absorption peak and the best excitation wavelength of OI-FMs ( Fig. 2a) are located at 360 nm. When excited by 360 nm UV light, the maximum emission wavelength is about 440 nm, which is similar with CA-AEAPMDS [21]. When the excitation wavelength changes (280–400 nm), as shown in Fig. 2b, emission peaks are excitation-independent and remain at 440 nm, which suggests that the distribution of fluorescence molecules contained in OI-FMs should be uniformly distributed interiorly and exteriorly [28,29].

As shown in Fig. 3, temperature has a great influence on the fluorescence intensity of OI-FMs within a certain temperature range. With increasing of temperature, the fluorescence intensity of OI-FMs firstly increases and then decreases, accompanying by redshift emission peaks, while morphology of OI-FMs does not change much (Fig. 3, S6 and S7). 120 °C is the optimal temperature for fluorescence intensity, and the fluorescence quantum yield was as high as 0.7 with emission peak red-shifting to 450 nm. Furthermore, the results of the heat treatment under vacuum conditions are the same, indicating that oxygen in the atmosphere has no effect on its fluorescence properties. As shown in Fig. S4, a significant potential change occurs in each functional group of the fluorescent center. Therefore, we speculate that the heating process causes electron transfer in the fluorescence center, resulting in a change in fluorescence intensity and a red shift in the emission peak.

Furthermore, compared to the CA-AEAPMDS aqueous solution, OI-FMs are not easily photobleached (Fig. S8), as reported in the literature [9,10], for the minimized movement of dye molecules due to their incorporation with silica matrix.

The effect of pH in phosphate buffer solution on photoluminescence intensity of OI-FMs-120 is showed in Fig. S9a. It can be clearly seen that the photoluminescence intensity of OI-FMs-120 remains constant over the pH range of 6–10, indicating that it is not too sensitive to pH value which is important for biosensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ in aqueous medium
Optimizing the experimental conditions for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the performance of OI-FMs-120 sensor for Hg2+, pH and incubation time was optimized. First, pH was optimized. As shown in Figure S9a, the fluorescence quenching value ΔF = F0-F (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively) increased firstly and then decreased in the range of 3.0–10.0, the best quenching value was obtained at pH = 7.0. So buffer solution with a pH of 7 was used in this experiment. These results can be explained as follows. At low pH, hydrated Hg2+ are stable under acidic conditions, and Hg2+ reacts with OH- to form Hg(OH)2 at higher pH level, which is easily transformed into HgO precipitation. In addition, as shown in Figure S9b, the quenched effect remains stable after 2 min. Therefore, 2 min is set as the best incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of OI-FMs-120 for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of OI-FMs-120 for metal ions was evaluated at pH 7 for 2 min (Fig. S9b). Relative fluorescence intensity to different metal ions were measured in water ( Fig. 4, blue bars). The results indicated that fluorescence of OI-FMs-120 is highly quenched by Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions, but not to other metal ions listed in Fig. 4. That is, OI-FMs-120 is highly selective for Hg2+ after removal of Fe3+ with an appropriate masking agent [30]. As shown in Fig. S10, sodium hexametaphosphate (SHPP) perfectly shielded ferric ions without affecting mercury ion detection. Furthermore, competition experiments were conducted in the presence of Hg2+ and other metal ions (Fig. 4, green bars), the bindings between OI-FMs-120 and Hg2+ were not affected significantly by other metal ions. The spatial distribution of amide groups on fluorescent molecules and the secondary amine groups in the middle of the molecular chain formed two binding sites capturing both Fe3+ and Hg2+ to form a complex [31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of OI-FMs-120 for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensitivity of the synthesized OI-FMs-120 towards Hg2+ was analyzed by fluorescence titrations. From Fig. 5a, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of OI-FMs-120 is gradually quenched with increasing concentration of Hg2+ (0–50 μM). We found that fluorescence quenching of this system followed the Stern-Volmer equation [33].
I 0
/ I = 1 +
K SV
Q
Where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, and [Q] is the quencher concentration. Quenching data are usually presented as plots of I0/I versus [Q]. The Stern-Volmer plot shown in Fig. 5b is not linear calibration plots over the whole Hg2+ concentration range from 0 to 50 μM, due to the presence of both static and dynamic mechanisms as observed by other researchers [34,35]. In contrast, inset of Fig. 5b reveals that a linear calibration plot can be fitted between I0/I and the concentration of Hg2+ at the range from 0 to 45 μM, with a linear regression equation of I0/I = 0.0602 [Hg2+] + 0.9695 (μM) (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9977, n = 7). The detection limit of 34.8 nM was obtained based on a 3δ/m (δ is the standard deviation of the blank OI-FMs-120 sample signal (n = 7) and m is the slope of the linear calibration plot), which was lower than most of the previous reported assays for Hg2+ detection (shown in Table 1). Thus, this method has high sensitivity and high linear response range for Hg2+ detection.

In order to explore the practicality of OI-FMs-120, a treated tap water and river water samples was used to prepare test solution containing different Hg2+ concentrations, and performed Hg2+ detection. The results were shown in Table 2. The data shows that there was good agreement between the found and the added concentrations of Hg2+. The recovery rates were between 98.9% and 103.3%. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of both methods was less than 1.66%. Therefore, the present method could effectively determine Hg2+ in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the detection of biothiols
ce:section.ce:para: It is reported that a heavy metal-thiol bond is easily formed between a thiol group in biothiols and heavy metal ions, and the bonding strength of this bond is much stronger than electrostatic interaction [42,43]. For OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ system, the formed chemical bonds between Hg2+ and OI-FMs-120 were broken with the addition of biothiol, resulting in fluorescence recovery as described in Scheme 1. Fluorescence spectrum of the sensing system was studied to verify the detection mechanism. As shown in Fig. 5a, with the continuous addition of Hg2+, the strong emission peak of OI-FMs-120 was significantly lowered near 450 nm. However, after the addition of the biothiol, the peak belonging to OI-FMs-120 was markedly restored ( Fig. 6). The explanation for the fluorescence enhancement process is that the formation of heavy metal-thiol bonds disturbs the interaction between Hg2+ and OI-FMs-120, causing the mercury ions to detach from the surface of OI-FMs-120 and resulting in OI-FMs-120 fluorescence recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ system for biothiols
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ for biothiols detection was evaluated. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 6a (green bars) that only thiol-containing amino acids (Cys, Hcy, GSH) lead to enhanced fluorescence of OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ system, and other thiol-free amino acids have little effect on the fluorescence of the system. These results indicate that OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ system shows very good specificity for biothiols. In addition, in order to study the anti-interference of the system to biothiols, we studied the potential interference effects of amino acids above, and the results were shown in Fig. 6a (purple bars). The effect of these thiol-free amino acids on the detection of biothiols by OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ system is limited. Thus, OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ can be explored as a highly selective sensing system for biothiols with good anti-interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ system for biothiols
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of OI-FMs-120-Hg2+system to Cys was investigated in the presence of increasing Cys concentration. As displayed in Fig. 7a, as Cys concentration increased, fluorescence intensity of the system increased gradually. The plot of the fluorescence enhanced against Cys concentration in Fig. 7b. It is clear that fluorescence intensity exhibits a linear response to Cys concentration in the range of 0–25 μM (R2 = 0.9989, n = 6), and the calibration curve can be express as I0/I= 0.03079 [Cys] + 0.9966 (μM). Here, I0 and I are fluorescence intensities of OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ in the presence and absence of Cys, respectively. The detection limit of 91.0 nM was obtained.

As Cys concentration increased, the recovery rate was 85% compared to the original solution (Fig. 6b). In addition, similar to Cys, GSH and Hcy also cause fluorescence recovery in varying degrees. The detection limit are as low as 0.12 μM for Hcy and 0.13 μM for GSH (Fig. S11). These results show that this sensing system shows excellent selectivity for biothiols.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ in anhydrous ethanol
Selectivity of OI-FMs-120 for Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The pH cannot be adjusted in absolute ethanol, and we still choose two minutes for the best incubation time. The selectivity of OI-FMs-120 for metal ions was evaluated. In Fig. 8, black bars clearly show that fluorescence of OI-FMs-120 is only highly quenched by Fe3+, and other metal ions have little effect on its fluorescence. It is proved that OI-FMs-120 has high selectivity to Fe3+. Furthermore, competition experiments were conducted in the presence of Fe3+ with other metal ions (Fig. 8, red bars). It can be seen that fluorescence of OI-FMs-120 after binding to Fe3+ does not change significantly in the presence of other metal ions. In previous work, our prepared CA-AEAPMDS [21] was not selective to Fe3+, while OI-FMs-120 shows good selectivity and sensitivity to Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of OI-FMs-120 for Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of synthesized OI-FMs-120 towards Fe3+ was analyzed by fluorescence titration. From Fig. 9a, fluorescence intensity of OI-FMs-120 is gradually quenched with increasing Hg2+ concentration from 0 to 75 μM. Insert of Fig. 9b reveals a linear calibration plot between I0/I and Hg2+ concentration at the range from 0 to 45 μM and the linear regression equation is I0/I = 0.05545 [Fe3+] + 0.952 (μM) (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9976, n = 10). The detection limit for Fe3+ is 37.8 nM, which is comparable or even better than those reported parameters in the literature (shown in Table 3). The method has high sensitivity and high linear response range for Fe3+ detection and can be used as one of the candidate materials for detecting Fe3+.

The results of practical exploration of tap water and river water were shown in Table 4. The data shows good agreement between the found and added Fe3+ concentrations. The recovery rates were between 98.5% and 103.9%. The RSD of two measurements was less than 1.82%. The results clearly demonstrate the accuracy and reliability of the sensor in detecting Fe3+ in actual water samples.

The typical transition metal ions such as Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+ can quench the fluorescence of CA-AEAPMDS in aqueous media [21], but OI-FMs-120 only shows good selectivity and sensitivity to Fe3+ and Hg2+ in aqueous media. It was discovered that N and O elements in XPS plot undergone electron transfer in some degree. Therefore, we speculate that the heating process causes electron transfer between them, which forming a special structure of oxygen and nitrogen lone pairs [17]. That is, the spatial distribution of the amide group in OI-FMs-120 and the amine group in the middle of the fluorescent molecule are likely to form two binding site-specific captures of Fe3+ and Hg2+ and form complex. This combination may be due to non-radiative electrons’ hole-recombination annihilation via an efficient electron-transfer process, causing fluorescence quenching. When anhydrous ethanol is used as a dispersion medium, OI-FMs-120 loses its ability to respond to Hg2+, and only Fe3+ can quench its fluorescence, which makes it an excellent probe for the specific detection of Fe3+ with anhydrous ethanol as the medium. We speculate that the presence of aqueous medium is the reason that OI-FMs-120 can detect Hg2+ and use experiments to prove this point: A certain amount of sample molecules were separately dispersed into absolute ethanol and aqueous solution to make the fluorescence intensity of the two solutions the same, and then an equal amount of mercury ions were separately added, and the mixture was shaken well to perform fluorescence detection. After three repeated tests, it was found that the sample can only be quenched by Hg2+ when dispersed in aqueous media, but not in anhydrous ethanol (Fig. S12). Therefore, we speculate that this may be due to the formation of a special structure between water molecules and OI-FMs-120, which provides a site for binding Hg2+.

In brief, an organic-inorganic hybrid fluorescent microsphere (OI-FMs) without typical fluorophores with a specific surface area of 95.02 m2/g and an average pore size of about 1.006 nm was prepared. We found that preparation temperature has a significant effect on fluorescence intensity of fluorescent microspheres. OI-FMs has the highest fluorescence quantum yield of up to 70.0% when heated in a 120 °C oven (named as OI-FMs-120). Interestingly, OI-FMs-120 is capable of selective detection for Fe3+ and Hg2+ in aqueous media. More surprisingly, when anhydrous ethanol is used as a dispersion medium, OI-FMs-120 does not respond to Hg2+, and only Fe3+ can quench its fluorescence, this peculiar property remains after three repeated treatments. In addition, OI-FMs-120-Hg2+ system can selectively detect sulfhydryl-containing amino acids due to the strong binding capacity between Hg2+ and -SH. As the biothiols concentration increases, the chemical bonds formed between Hg2+ and OI-FMs-120 break down, bringing about fluorescence recovery. Among three kinds of thiols, recovery rate of Cys was higher than those of GSH and Hcy. The possible reason is that Cys has a specific structure, leading to the preferential coordination with Hg2+.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (China) (ZR2015BL011).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.11.082.
